process: Traditional Ingot Metallurgy. A basic, non-stainless high-carbon steel known for its simplicity and ability to take a very keen edge.

name: 10Cr15MoV
process: Ingot Metallurgy. A Chinese high-carbon stainless steel that serves as a direct equivalent to Japanese VG10, utilizing Cobalt to increase hardness.

name: 12C27
process: Ingot Metallurgy by Sandvik. A high-purity steel with a fine carbide structure, optimized for razor-like sharpness and high corrosion resistance.

name: 140Cr
process: Laminated Ingot Metallurgy. Often used by SRM, this is a complex alloy using Niobium and Vanadium to create fine, hard carbides for improved wear resistance.

name: 14C28N
process: Ingot Metallurgy by Sandvik. Nitrogen-enriched to allow for high hardness and corrosion resistance without compromising the fine grain structure.

name: 154CM
process: Ingot Metallurgy. Originally developed for jet engine valves, it adds Molybdenum to a 440C base to improve edge retention and toughness.

name: 420HC
process: Ingot Metallurgy. A high-carbon version of 420 stainless; when heat-treated correctly (famous by Buck Knives), it is very tough and easy to maintain.

name: 440C
process: Ingot Metallurgy. The classic 'high-end' steel of the 20th century. It features large chromium carbides that provide good wear resistance but lower toughness.

name: 8Cr13MoV
process: Ingot Metallurgy. A prolific Chinese budget steel similar to AUS-8. It offers a balanced performance for everyday light tasks.

name: 9Cr18MoV
process: Ingot Metallurgy. Essentially a Chinese version of 440C with slightly better corrosion resistance due to the high Chromium content.

name: AEB-L
process: Ingot Metallurgy by Uddeholm. Originally a razor blade steel, it is prized by custom makers for its extremely fine carbide structure and high toughness.

name: AR-RPM9
process: Powder Metallurgy. A unique budget PM steel developed for Artisan Cutlery. It provides the clean structure of powder steel at an entry-level price point.

name: AUS10
process: Ingot Metallurgy by Aichi. A Japanese stainless steel that competes with 440C and 154CM, offering a slightly finer grain and better sharpenability.

name: AUS8
process: Ingot Metallurgy by Aichi. A time-tested Japanese steel known for taking a very fine edge very quickly; common in high-quality budget folders.

name: CPM-154
process: Powder Metallurgy by Crucible. Uses the exact chemistry of 154CM but processed into powder to create much smaller carbides and significantly higher toughness.

name: CPM-20CV
process: Powder Metallurgy by Crucible. The American version of M390. Extremely high chromium and vanadium content for massive wear resistance and rust proofing.

name: CPM-3V
process: Crucible Particle Metallurgy (CPM) tool steel designed to provide maximum resistance to breakage and chipping. It approaches the impact toughness of S7 shock steel while maintaining wear resistance similar to D2.

name: CPM-CruWear
process: Powder Metallurgy by Crucible. An air-hardening tool steel designed to bridge the gap between toughness-focused steels and wear-resistance steels.

name: CPM-M4
process: Powder Metallurgy by Crucible. A high-speed tool steel with very high Tungsten and Vanadium. It is a favorite for competitive cutting due to its impact resistance.

name: CPM S10V
process: Crucible Particle Metallurgy (CPM) process. Designed for extreme wear resistance in industrial tooling and high-end custom cutting tools.

name: CPM-S30V
process: Powder Metallurgy by Crucible. The first steel ever developed specifically for the cutlery industry, balancing edge holding and corrosion resistance.

name: CPM-S35VN
process: Powder Metallurgy by Crucible. An evolution of S30V that adds Niobium to make the steel tougher and easier for manufacturers to grind and polish.

name: CPM-S45VN
process: Powder Metallurgy by Crucible. A further refinement of S35VN with increased Chromium and Nitrogen to push corrosion resistance into the elite category.

name: CPM-S90V
process: Powder Metallurgy by Crucible. High vanadium content creates extremely hard carbides. It is considered one of the kings of long-term edge retention.

name: CPM-S110V
process: Powder Metallurgy by Crucible. One of the most alloy-rich steels available. It uses Niobium and Vanadium to create an edge that lasts almost indefinitely.

name: CTS BD1N
process: Vacuum Melted stainless steel by Carpenter Technology. The 'N' signifies the addition of Nitrogen, which increases hardness and corrosion resistance without making the steel brittle.

name: CTS-XHP
process: Powder Metallurgy by Carpenter. Often described as 'Stainless D2,' it offers the edge retention of a tool steel with the stain resistance of a stainless steel.

name: D2
process: Ingot Metallurgy. A semi-stainless tool steel. Its large carbides make it a 'toothy' cutter, but it requires oil to prevent rust.

name: ELMAX
process: Powder Metallurgy by Böhler. A high-performance European steel known for its high purity and the ability to hold a very fine, polished edge.

name: K110
process: Ingot Metallurgy by Böhler. This is essentially a high-quality, refined version of D2 tool steel produced in Europe.

name: K390
process: Powder Metallurgy by Böhler. An industrial cold-work tool steel that has become a legend for having incredible edge retention while remaining reasonably tough.

name: L220C
process: Ingot Metallurgy. A high-carbon silver steel variant. It is simple, clean, and often used in traditional or European-style working knives.

name: LC200N
process: PESR (Pressurized Electroslag Remelting). A nitrogen-based steel that is virtually rust-proof; it replaces carbon with nitrogen to prevent corrosion.

name: M390
process: Powder Metallurgy (Microclean) by Böhler. Widely considered one of the best all-around super steels for high-end 'jewelry' and EDC knives.

name: M398
process: A powder metallurgy evolution of M390 by Böhler. By increasing Carbon and Vanadium, Böhler achieved a carbide volume of approx. 30%, resulting in extreme wear resistance and a potential hardness of 63-66 HRC.

name: MagnaCut
process: Powder Metallurgy by Crucible. A breakthrough steel designed by Dr. Larrin Thomas to eliminate large Chromium carbides, resulting in extreme toughness and stainlessness.

name: Maxamet
process: Powder Metallurgy by Carpenter. A super-hard tool steel that borders on cemented carbide. It is extremely difficult to work with but holds an edge for ages.

name: N690
process: Ingot Metallurgy by Böhler. A cobalt-enriched stainless steel from Austria. It is very popular with European makers for its ease of sharpening and high polish.

name: Nitro-V
process: Ingot Metallurgy. An evolution of AEB-L that adds Nitrogen and Vanadium to improve corrosion resistance and edge stability.

name: Nitro-X7
process: A high-tech nitrogen-alloyed stainless steel by Buderus Edelstahl. It utilizes a fine-grained 'Nitro-Concept' to achieve edge stability and hardness (up to 64 HRC) that rivals powder metallurgy steels without the PM price tag.

name: Vanax
process: Powder Metallurgy by Uddeholm. A 'Super-Stainless' steel where Nitrogen almost entirely replaces Carbon, making it immune to salt-water rust.

name: VG10
process: Ingot Metallurgy by Takefu. Known as 'Gold Steel' in Japan, it is a high-standard stainless that is world-famous for its use in professional kitchen cutlery.
